Sweden.grid_connection_approval: Elnat (local grid operator), typically 4 to 6 weeks
Sweden.small_farm_notes: Biogas plants under 50 kW qualify for simplified grid connection. Green electricity certificates add value.
Sweden.subsidy_available: Investment grants up to 30% via Energimyndigheten
Sweden.self_consumption_benefit: Avoided cost of €0.14/kWh plus certificate value

UK.grid_connection_approval: Your local Distribution Network Operator (DNO), typically 8 to 12 weeks
UK.small_farm_notes: SEG replaced Feed-in Tariff in 2020. Any grid-connected generator under 5 MW can apply. Rates vary by supplier.
UK.subsidy_available: Farming in Protected Landscapes grants, rural grant schemes
UK.self_consumption_benefit: Avoided cost of £0.28/kWh is more valuable than export

Germany.grid_connection_approval: Local Netzbetreiber (grid operator), typically 4 to 8 weeks
Germany.small_farm_notes: Biogas plants under 75 kW: €0.09 to €0.15/kWh guaranteed 20 years. Flexibility bonus €100/kW raised Jan 2025. 1,300 MW biogas tenders in 2025.
Germany.subsidy_available: BAFA grants, KfW low interest loans for renewables
Germany.self_consumption_benefit: Avoided cost of €0.28/kWh plus tax exemption on self-produced power

France.small_farm_notes: Solar under 9 kW: €0.20/kWh. 9 to 100 kW: €0.14/kWh. 20-year contracts. Rates adjusted quarterly. COSEC certificate required.
France.subsidy_available: MaPrimeRénov, ADEME grants for agricultural biogas
France.self_consumption_benefit: Avoided cost of €0.20/kWh. Self-consumption priority recommended

Spain.grid_connection_approval: Local distributor (Iberdrola, Endesa, etc.), typically 4 to 8 weeks
Spain.small_farm_notes: Self-consumption (autoconsumo) strongly favored. Surplus sold at market rate ~€0.05-0.08/kWh. The 'sun tax' was abolished in 2018. Simplified procedure for installations under 100 kW.
Spain.subsidy_available: IDAE grants, EU Recovery funds for agricultural renewables, regional subsidies
Spain.self_consumption_benefit: Avoided cost of €0.18/kWh. Almost always better than export

Italy.export_scheme: FER X Decree (February 2025) + Scambio sul Posto
Italy.grid_connection_approval: Gestore Servizi Energetici (GSE), typically 6 to 12 weeks
Italy.small_farm_notes: FER X decree (Feb 2025): direct access for plants under 1 MW. Up to 40% grants for small enterprises. Tax credits up to 150% for agricultural biogas. Scambio sul Posto allows net metering for plants under 200 kW.
Italy.subsidy_available: Piano Agricoltura, PNRR funds, 40% capital grant for biogas under 300 kW
Italy.self_consumption_benefit: Avoided cost of €0.25/kWh plus Scambio sul Posto offset

Netherlands.grid_connection_approval: Netbeheerder (Stedin, Enexis, Liander), typically 6 to 16 weeks
Netherlands.small_farm_notes: Net metering ends January 2027. After that: export at ~€0.05-0.10/kWh. SDE++ subsidy available for agricultural biogas (up to 15 years guaranteed price). Grid congestion a real issue in rural areas — check with netbeheerder first.
Netherlands.subsidy_available: SDE++ (up to €0.11/kWh guaranteed for biogas), MEI grant for rural energy
Netherlands.self_consumption_benefit: 1:1 net metering offset until 2026. After 2027: self-consumption strongly preferred

Denmark.small_farm_notes: Biogas covered under the Danish biogas support scheme until 2027. Green certificate market adds value. Strong government support for agricultural biogas — Denmark aims to expand biogas use significantly.
Denmark.self_consumption_benefit: Avoided cost of €0.31/kWh — self-consumption highly valuable

Norway.small_farm_notes: Grid electricity is very cheap (€0.08/kWh) due to hydro dominance — savings from self-generation are lower in Norway. However grid CO2 is nearly zero (0.017 kg/kWh) so carbon credit value is minimal. Biogas is most valuable for digestate (fertilizer savings).
Norway.subsidy_available: Enova grants for agricultural energy efficiency
Norway.self_consumption_benefit: Limited — cheap grid power means lower avoided cost

Finland.small_farm_notes: Biogas premium scheme guarantees ~€0.075/kWh above market price. Cold climate means higher parasitic load (up to 30% in winter). Battery storage particularly important for energy independence.
Finland.subsidy_available: Business Finland grants, rural development funds (LEADER)

Austria.small_farm_notes: Renewable energy subsidy through OeMAG. Agricultural biogas under 500 kW eligible. Austrian Climate and Energy Fund provides additional grants.
Austria.subsidy_available: Klima- und Energiefonds grants, AWS loans
Austria.self_consumption_benefit: Avoided cost of €0.24/kWh — strong case for self-consumption

Czech Republic.grid_connection_approval: Distribution System Operator (DSO), typically 6 to 12 weeks
Czech Republic.small_farm_notes: Czech ERU sets guaranteed prices for 15-20 years. High coal dependence means good carbon savings. Support primarily for biogas under 250 kW.
Czech Republic.subsidy_available: OPPIK grants, State Environmental Fund subsidies
Czech Republic.self_consumption_benefit: Avoided cost of €0.21/kWh plus grid reliability benefit

Poland.export_scheme: RES Act auction system + FiT for small installations
Poland.grid_connection_approval: Local OSD (Operator Systemu Dystrybucyjnego), typically 8 to 16 weeks
Poland.small_farm_notes: Very high grid CO2 (0.618 kg/kWh) means largest carbon credit potential in Europe. Micro-installation FiT for farms under 50 kW. Auction system for larger plants. Grid connection can be slow — plan ahead.
Poland.subsidy_available: NFOSiGW grants, EU Cohesion Fund, KPO (National Recovery Plan) for agricultural biogas
Poland.self_consumption_benefit: Strongest carbon argument in Europe. €0.19/kWh avoided plus massive CO2 credits

Belgium.export_scheme: Green certificate system + VREG regulated net metering
Belgium.grid_connection_approval: Fluvius (Flanders) or Sibelga (Brussels), typically 6 to 10 weeks
Belgium.small_farm_notes: Green certificate system rewards renewable production. Regional differences: Flanders, Wallonia, and Brussels have separate schemes. Net metering for residential and small commercial.
Belgium.subsidy_available: VLAIO investment support, Walloon REI program

Switzerland.grid_connection_approval: Local grid operator, typically 4 to 8 weeks
Switzerland.small_farm_notes: KEV subsidy has a waiting list — early registration is key. One-time investment subsidy also available as an alternative. CH-origin guarantee certificates add value.
Switzerland.subsidy_available: Bundesamt fur Energie (SFOE) one-time investment subsidies up to 30%
Switzerland.self_consumption_benefit: Avoided cost of €0.23/kWh — strong self-consumption case

Ireland.export_scheme: SSRH (Small-Scale Renewable Electricity Support Scheme) + Biomethane Strategy
Ireland.small_farm_notes: Ireland has an operational tariff for biogas CHP. Pilot scheme for 15 farm-scale biogas plants. Government target: 5.7 TWh of biomethane by 2030 — strong policy support expected.
Ireland.subsidy_available: SEAI grants for agricultural energy, TAMS III farm scheme
Ireland.self_consumption_benefit: Avoided cost of €0.26/kWh — very strong case for self-consumption

Portugal.grid_connection_approval: E-Redes or local DSO, typically 6 to 10 weeks
Portugal.small_farm_notes: UPAC regime strongly promotes self-consumption. Very high solar resource (5.5 kWh/m²/day in Lisbon). Surplus can be sold under UPP small producer regime.
Portugal.subsidy_available: PRR (Plano de Recuperação e Resiliência) agriculture grants, POSEUR program
Portugal.self_consumption_benefit: Avoided cost of €0.22/kWh. Solar self-consumption particularly strong at 5.5 kWh/m²/day

Greece.grid_connection_approval: HEDNO (Hellenic Electricity Distribution Network Operator), typically 8 to 14 weeks
Greece.small_farm_notes: Net metering for installations under 500 kW. Highest solar irradiance in Europe (5.8 kWh/m²/day in Athens). Agriculture net-metering allows offsetting agricultural consumption from on-farm generation.
Greece.subsidy_available: ESIF grants for agricultural energy, National Development Plan
Greece.self_consumption_benefit: Avoided cost of €0.19/kWh plus exceptional solar resource

general_disclaimer: All regulatory information is for guidance only. Actual feed-in tariffs, connection requirements, grid capacity, and subsidy availability must be confirmed with your local grid operator and national energy regulator. Tariff rates change frequently. Always consult a qualified energy engineer before making investment decisions.
